verses.commentary: 'In those days' — vague time-jump from infancy to adulthood. John appears in the wilderness (eremos, ἔρημος), the place of Israel's formation under Moses. New exodus geography.

'Repent (metanoeite, μετανοεῖτε), for the kingdom of heaven has come near.' Identical to Jesus' opening message (4:17). Repentance is not penance but mind-change. The kingdom is imminent — proximity demands response.
Isaiah 40:3 quoted. The voice in the wilderness prepares the way of YHWH. Matthew applies YHWH's coming to Jesus' coming — high Christology embedded in citation.

John dresses like Elijah (2 Kings 1:8): camel hair, leather belt. Wilderness diet of locusts and wild honey. Malachi 4:5 promised Elijah's return before the day of the Lord — John is that forerunner (11:14).
Mass movement. Jerusalem, Judea, the Jordan region — the whole nation stirs. Confession precedes baptism: public acknowledgment of sin.
Jewish ritual washings existed (mikveh) but baptism by another, in the Jordan, demanding repentance — this is new. Even the covenant people need cleansing. Birth-status is not enough.

'Brood of vipers' (gennemata echidnon, γεννήματα ἐχιδνῶν) — children of snakes (echoing Genesis 3:15). John identifies the religious elite with the serpent. The wrath (orge, ὀργή) approaches; even Pharisees cannot escape on pedigree.
Fruit (karpos, καρπός) — repentance must be visible, behavioral, demonstrable. Internal sincerity proven externally. The test will recur throughout Matthew (7:16-20; 12:33).
Abrahamic descent will not save them. God can raise children of Abraham from stones (echoing Ezekiel 36:26 — hearts of stone replaced). Membership is not biological.
The ax already laid at the root. Judgment is imminent and total. Trees without fruit go to the fire — eschatological warning preceding gospel.

John's water baptism only symbolizes; the coming one baptizes with Holy Spirit and fire — purification (Joel 2:28-29) and judgment (Malachi 3:2). Removing sandals (a slave's task) John is unworthy to do. Radical deference.

Winnowing fork (ptyon, πτύον) separates wheat from chaff. The Messiah's work is judicial sorting: gathered into barn or burned with unquenchable fire (pyr asbeston, πῦρ ἄσβεστον). Final, not penultimate.
Jesus travels from Galilee to be baptized. The sinless one comes to a baptism for repentance. Identification with sinners begins his public ministry.
John protests — recognizes the inversion. The lesser baptizes the greater. Even John, knowing Jesus' identity, doesn't yet understand the necessity of substitution.

'To fulfill all righteousness' (plerosai pasan dikaiosynen, πληρῶσαι πᾶσαν δικαιοσύνην). Jesus does not need cleansing — he submits to identify with the people he will save. Solidarity precedes substitution.

Heavens opened (anoigo, ἀνοίγω; Mark uses violent schizo). Spirit descends 'like a dove' — recalling creation hovering (Genesis 1:2) and Noah's dove signaling new world (Genesis 8). New creation inaugurated visibly.

The Father's voice. 'This is my Son' (Psalm 2:7, royal enthronement), 'whom I love' (Genesis 22:2, the beloved son Isaac), 'with whom I am well pleased' (Isaiah 42:1, the Servant). Three OT echoes converge: King, Sacrifice, Servant. Identity declared before any ministry occurs.
